title: The colleague who remembers everyone's name
summary: Describe a colleague whose unusual skill is remembering everyone's name and small details about them, and why this quiet talent matters more than it looks.
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about a colleague who reliably remembers everyone's name and small personal details, and what their quiet attention reveals about respect and workplace culture. Use the present perfect for accumulated observation, a second conditional, modals, and 'although' or 'however'.
hintStarters: There is a colleague at our office in Penang named Aishah who, on her first day, somehow learned the names of all twenty-seven people on our floor, including two contractors who only came in on Fridays, and over six years she has…
If a name is the smallest thing one adult can offer another, then forgetting it is one of the smallest disrespects, which is something most of us…

Although remembering names sounds trivial, what I have come to understand from watching Aishah is that she is quietly doing a piece of workplace maintenance that the official organisation chart cannot…

hintVocab.meaningVi: khiến ai cảm thấy được nhìn thấy

hintVocab.phrase: to put a name to a face
hintVocab.meaningVi: ghép tên với một khuôn mặt
một nơi làm việc bao trùm

sampleText:
There is a colleague at our office in Penang named Aishah who, on her first day, somehow learned the names of all twenty-seven people on our floor, including two contractors who only came in on Fridays. Six years later, she still knows whose mother had surgery, whose teenager is preparing for an exam, and which colleague does not like to be greeted before their first coffee. I have watched new staff arrive expecting the usual mild anonymity of a mid-sized office and be visibly disarmed by her, in the first week, asking them by name about something they had mentioned once in passing. The effect is small in any single interaction. Across six years it has produced one of the few workplaces I have ever been part of where almost no one feels invisible.

If a name is the smallest thing one adult can offer another, then forgetting it is one of the smallest disrespects, and yet most of us commit it daily without noticing. There is a particular type of small social pain that comes from realising a colleague you have worked next to for two years still does not know how to spell your name and has been avoiding writing it. Aishah's quiet talent removes that pain wherever she goes in the building. She is not noticeably warmer or louder than other colleagues. She is just paying attention. Although remembering names sounds trivial, what I have come to understand from watching her is that she is doing a piece of workplace maintenance the official organisation chart cannot describe. The chart shows who reports to whom; it does not show who feels welcomed each morning. She is quietly responsible for a significant fraction of the latter.

What I find most useful about her example is that the skill, when you study it, is not magical. She writes names down on the day she meets people. She asks twice if she did not catch a name the first time, instead of pretending she did. She uses someone's name in the first three sentences of a conversation, which makes it much harder to forget. None of this is hidden. Anyone could do it. However, almost no one does, because it requires you to treat every introduction as worth a small amount of effort, and most of us, in a normal busy week, do not. The lesson, I think, is not that workplaces need 'an Aishah' — that would be unfair to her, since she would then be doing emotional labour for the whole building. The lesson is that small, almost invisible acts of attention add up to a kind of culture, and that anyone willing to copy three or four of her habits would quietly make their corner of the office a slightly better place to spend a day.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional with passive: 'would + be + -ing'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'That would be unfair to her, since she would then be doing emotional labour for the whole building' dùng điều kiện loại 2 với 'would + be + -ing' (tiếp diễn) để diễn đạt một kết quả giả định mà vẫn đang diễn ra trong tình huống tưởng tượng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'That would be unfair to her, since she would then be doing emotional labour for the whole building' uses the second conditional with 'would + be + -ing' (continuous) to express a hypothetical result that would be ongoing in the imagined situation.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for accumulated observation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I have watched new staff arrive...', 'I have ever been part of', 'What I have come to understand' đều dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành để tổng hợp quan sát qua thời gian — đặc biệt thích hợp khi viết về một xu hướng hoặc đặc điểm lâu dài.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I have watched new staff arrive...', 'I have ever been part of', 'What I have come to understand' all use the present perfect to summarise observation over time — especially appropriate when writing about a long-running tendency or feature.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một đồng nghiệp ở văn phòng chúng tôi ở Penang tên Aishah, vào ngày đầu tiên của cô ấy, bằng cách nào đó đã học tên của tất cả hai mươi bảy người trên tầng chúng tôi, bao gồm hai nhà thầu chỉ đến vào thứ Sáu. Sáu năm sau, cô ấy vẫn biết mẹ ai vừa phẫu thuật, con tuổi teen của ai đang chuẩn bị cho một kỳ thi, và đồng nghiệp nào không thích được chào trước cốc cà phê đầu tiên. Tôi đã xem nhân viên mới đến mong đợi sự vô danh nhẹ thông thường của một văn phòng cỡ trung và bị giải giáp rõ rệt bởi cô ấy, trong tuần đầu tiên, hỏi họ bằng tên về điều gì đó họ đã đề cập một lần thoáng qua. Hiệu ứng nhỏ trong bất kỳ tương tác đơn lẻ nào. Qua sáu năm nó đã tạo ra một trong số ít nơi làm việc tôi từng là một phần nơi gần như không ai cảm thấy vô hình.

Nếu một cái tên là điều nhỏ nhất một người lớn có thể cho người lớn khác, thì quên nó là một trong những sự bất kính nhỏ nhất, vậy mà hầu hết chúng ta phạm nó hàng ngày mà không nhận ra. Có một loại đau xã hội nhỏ đặc biệt đến từ việc nhận ra rằng một đồng nghiệp bạn đã làm việc kề bên trong hai năm vẫn không biết cách đánh vần tên bạn và đã tránh viết nó. Tài năng thầm lặng của Aishah loại bỏ nỗi đau đó ở bất cứ nơi nào cô đi trong tòa nhà. Cô ấy không ấm áp hơn hay ồn ào hơn các đồng nghiệp khác một cách rõ rệt. Cô ấy chỉ đang chú ý. Mặc dù nhớ tên nghe có vẻ tầm thường, điều tôi đã dần hiểu từ việc xem cô là cô đang làm một phần bảo trì nơi làm việc mà biểu đồ tổ chức chính thức không thể mô tả. Biểu đồ cho thấy ai báo cáo cho ai; nó không cho thấy ai cảm thấy được chào đón mỗi sáng. Cô ấy lặng lẽ chịu trách nhiệm cho một phần đáng kể của điều thứ hai.

Điều tôi thấy hữu ích nhất về ví dụ của cô là kỹ năng, khi bạn nghiên cứu nó, không phải là phép thuật. Cô ấy ghi tên xuống vào ngày cô gặp người ta. Cô ấy hỏi hai lần nếu cô không nghe rõ tên lần đầu tiên, thay vì giả vờ đã nghe. Cô ấy dùng tên ai đó trong ba câu đầu tiên của một cuộc trò chuyện, điều làm cho việc quên khó hơn nhiều. Không có điều nào trong số này bị giấu. Bất kỳ ai cũng có thể làm. Tuy nhiên, gần như không ai làm, vì nó yêu cầu bạn đối xử với mọi lời giới thiệu như đáng giá một chút nỗ lực, và hầu hết chúng ta, trong một tuần bận bình thường, không làm vậy. Bài học, tôi nghĩ, không phải là nơi làm việc cần 'một Aishah' — điều đó sẽ không công bằng với cô ấy, vì khi đó cô sẽ làm lao động cảm xúc cho cả tòa nhà. Bài học là những hành động chú ý nhỏ, gần như vô hình tích lại thành một loại văn hóa, và bất kỳ ai sẵn lòng sao chép ba bốn thói quen của cô sẽ lặng lẽ làm cho góc văn phòng của họ thành một nơi tốt hơn một chút để dành một ngày.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses present perfect correctly?
options: Across six years it has produced one of the few workplaces I have ever been part of where almost no one feels invisible.
Across six years it have produced one of the few workplaces I have ever been part of where almost no one feels invisible.
Across six years it has produce one of the few workplaces I have ever been part of where almost no one feels invisible.
Across six years it has produced one of the few workplaces I has ever been part of where almost no one feels invisible.
mcQuestions.explanation: Singular subject 'it' → 'has produced' (present perfect with past participle); 'I have ever been part of' is also correct present perfect.
mcQuestions.hint: 'It has + past participle'; 'I have + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the second conditional correctly?
options: That would be unfair to her, since she would then be doing emotional labour for the whole building.
That will be unfair to her, since she would then be doing emotional labour for the whole building.
That would be unfair to her, since she would then to do emotional labour for the whole building.
That would be unfair to her, since she would then being doing emotional labour for the whole building.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional with continuous: 'would + be + -ing' for a hypothetical ongoing result.

options: Although remembering names sounds trivial, she is doing a piece of workplace maintenance the official organisation chart cannot describe.
Although remembering names sounds trivial, but she is doing a piece of workplace maintenance the official organisation chart cannot describe.
Although, remembering names sounds trivial she is doing a piece of workplace maintenance the official organisation chart cannot describe.
Remembering names sounds trivial although however she is doing a piece of workplace maintenance the official organisation chart cannot describe.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling.

options: However, almost no one does, because it requires effort.
However almost no one does, because it requires effort.
However; almost no one does, because it requires effort.
However, almost no one do, because it requires effort.
mcQuestions.explanation: Sentence-initial 'however' takes a comma. 'No one' is singular → 'does' (not 'do').

options: She asks twice if she did not catch a name the first time, instead of pretending she did.
She asks twice if she did not catch a name the first time, instead of pretend she did.
She asks twice if she did not catch a name the first time, instead of to pretend she did.
She asks twice if she did not catch a name the first time, instead of pretended she did.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Instead of + -ing form' is the standard structure after the preposition.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'whose + noun' as a relative pronoun correctly?
options: She still knows whose mother had surgery and whose teenager is preparing for an exam.
She still knows who's mother had surgery and who's teenager is preparing for an exam.
She still knows whom mother had surgery and whom teenager is preparing for an exam.
She still knows that mother had surgery and that teenager is preparing for an exam.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whose + noun' is the possessive relative. 'Who's' is the contraction of 'who is'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'one of the few + plural noun + I have ever + past participle' correctly?
options: One of the few workplaces I have ever been part of where almost no one feels invisible.
One of the few workplace I have ever been part of where almost no one feels invisible.
One of few workplaces I have ever been part of where almost no one feels invisible.
One of the few workplaces I have ever be part of where almost no one feels invisible.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'One of the few + plural noun + (that) + I have ever + past participle' is the standard structure.
mcQuestions.hint: 'One of the few + plural noun'; 'have ever + been'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'make + object + adjective'?
options: She uses someone's name in the first three sentences, which makes it much harder to forget.
She uses someone's name in the first three sentences, which makes it much hard to forget.
She uses someone's name in the first three sentences, which makes it more harder to forget.
She uses someone's name in the first three sentences, which makes it much more hard to forget.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Make + it + comparative + to + base verb'; 'much harder' is the correct intensified comparative.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Make + it + much harder + to + base verb'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'be willing to + base verb' correctly?
options: Anyone willing to copy three or four of her habits would quietly make their corner of the office better.
Anyone willing copying three or four of her habits would quietly make their corner of the office better.
Anyone willing for copy three or four of her habits would quietly make their corner of the office better.
Anyone willing to copying three or four of her habits would quietly make their corner of the office better.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Willing to + base verb' is the standard structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'worth + a noun phrase' correctly?
options: It requires you to treat every introduction as worth a small amount of effort.
It requires you to treat every introduction as worth of a small amount of effort.
It requires you to treat every introduction as worth to a small amount of effort.
It requires you to treat every introduction as worth for a small amount of effort.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Worth + noun phrase' is the standard structure (also 'worth + -ing'). No 'of', 'to', or 'for'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'add up to + noun phrase' correctly?
options: Small, almost invisible acts of attention add up to a kind of culture.
Small, almost invisible acts of attention add up at a kind of culture.
Small, almost invisible acts of attention add up in a kind of culture.
Small, almost invisible acts of attention adds up to a kind of culture.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Add up to + noun' = result in / total to. Plural subject 'acts' takes 'add' (not 'adds').

criticalThinkingQuestion: If small acts of attention silently build a workplace culture, who should be expected to perform them? Is it a personality trait, a skill that managers should train, or unpaid emotional labour that quietly burdens the people who do it well?
